item.title: Prepare for an educational dunk on Cancun Ted Cruz in 3... 2... 1...
item.description: “Which slaves built Chicago?” — Ted Cruz Welcome to History 101: Things They Forgot to Teach You in School. Yes, Senator Cruz, you should sit in the front row. Lesson one: Chicago’s founder wasn’t a rugged white pioneer. It was Jean Baptiste Point du Sable, a free Black man from Haiti. In the 1780s, he set up a trading post, married a Potawatomi woman, and laid the foundation of the city. The “first family of Chicago” was Black and Indigenous. Sorry Ted, your pioneer fantasy just failed the midterm. Lesson two: Haiti’s Revolution (1791–1804). Enslaved Africans defeated Napoleon, declared the first free Black republic, and left France so broke it sold the Louisiana Territory to the U.S. Without Haiti, no Louisiana Purchase. Without the Purchase, no Chicago. That’s not “woke math” that’s arithmetic. Lesson three: Slavery financed Northern cities. By 1860, cotton picked by enslaved Africans made up nearly 60% of U.S. exports. Chicago’s warehouses, railroads, and banks thrived on that money. So no, enslaved people weren’t stacking bricks in Chicago but their labor paid for the bricks. Lesson four: Black labor built modern Chicago. From laying tracks and working stockyards to the Great Migration that created Bronzeville, Black hands built and rebuilt this city in every era. So when Cruz sneers, “Which slaves built Chicago?” the answer is simple: a Black Haitian founder, his Potawatomi wife, a Haitian slave revolt, an economy built on slavery, and generations of Black workers. And here’s a note from the professor: I’m not even Black. By America’s usual logic, I could shrug and say, “Doesn’t affect me.” But I do care. Because the more you learn, the more you understand and once you understand, you can’t unsee the truth. When you erase Black and Indigenous people from this story, you’re not just erasing them. You’re erasing America’s actual history. That’s not extra credit, Senator... that’s the whole course. Final exam: Chicago wasn’t built by rugged white settlers. It was built by the very people America enslaved, exploited, and then tried to erase from the story. Class dismissed. Senator Ted Cruz you didn’t just fail... you managed to drop the course, lose the textbook, and flunk summer school. Sauce: https://notsaintsnotsaviors.substack.com/p/which-slaves-built-chicago
item.dc:creator: Johje19

item.title: Yaaaaay!
item.description: I hope he suffers. Edit: He used to make my sister and me shower together as tweens, then burst into our rooms when we were still naked trying to get dressed. I put a lock on my bedroom door and it pissed him off so he removed my doorknob and I wasn't allowed to have one. My youngest sister (his daughter) moved in with him when she was 18 and he got drunk and tried to rape her saying, "You're my daughter, I can do whatever I want with you." He was wasted enough that she got away easily and she moved out that night. When she and my other sister (both his daughters) were very young and he got joint custody of them (mom got back together with him after a short time), he would crawl into bed with them in the middle of the night in nothing but his underwear and tell them he was lonely and couldn't sleep alone. When I was about nine or ten he was pummeling our mom and the neighbors called the cops. My sister and I tried to tell them what he's been doing and they said, "Be quiet, let the adults talk." Our mom denied that he was abusing her and they just left. We had it drilled into our heads not to talk to school counselors or else we'd be taken from our mom and never see her again. https://youtu.be/WMgpAq4JJB8?si=mECiPxQhZGR0Jv_V
item.dc:creator: Zinfandella

item.title: This is America
item.description: Note: I’m not tagging this as politics because this is now relevant to everyone who isn’t openly MAGA. Anyone of us can be disappeared by ICE, or the gangsters posing as ICE, there is no way for an ordinary citizen to tell the difference. Your trip to the hardware store can turn into a months long stay in some ICE facility. We are at the stage where going outside the house requires planning and preparation. If our rights can be taken away by an administration, then they weren’t really rights to begin with. (I’m fucking that quote up but I’m too pissed to look for it) I gotta do some planning of my own because I will be in the Chicago area for unrelated reasons. Stay safe out there.
item.dc:creator: DownvotesStarWars

Like what we do and how we do it? Leave a tip. Or don't. It's totally cool either way. Stay True & Stay Tuned - Meta Pathos

item.title: In the winter of 1925,the town of Nome, Alaska, faced a terrifying diphtheria outbreak. With more than 10,000 lives at risk, especially those of children, the only antitoxin available was in Anchorage — nearly 1,000 miles away. Planes couldn’t fly
item.description: In the winter of 1925, the town of Nome, Alaska, faced a terrifying diphtheria outbreak. With more than 10,000 lives at risk, especially those of children, the only antitoxin available was in Anchorage — nearly 1,000 miles away. Planes couldn’t fly in the brutal cold, and the nearest railway ended 674 miles from Nome. The solution fell to an extraordinary relay of sled dogs and their mushers. Among them was a small but fierce Siberian Husky named Togo, leading the team of Leonhard Seppala. At 12 years old, Togo ran the longest and most treacherous stretch of the entire mission: 264 miles across storms, breaking sea ice, and the black Arctic night. While other teams averaged about 31 miles each, Togo and Seppala carried the serum through the most dangerous terrain — a shortcut across the frozen Norton Sound. Togo’s journey was made even more remarkable by his unlikely beginnings. Sickly as a pup and weighing just 48 pounds as an adult, he was once considered too small and weak for sled work. Given away as a pet, he escaped by leaping through a closed window to return to his kennel, determined to run. He earned his place in the team after breaking out again to follow Seppala’s sled on a training run, logging an astounding 75 miles on his very first day in harness. Togo’s courage changed history. In just five and a half days, the serum reached Nome, saving countless lives. Though Balto would become more famous for completing the final leg, it was Togo who endured the hardest, longest, and most perilous part of the relay. Decades later, his legacy was finally recognized. In 2011, Time named Togo the most heroic animal in history. In 2019, Disney released the film Togo starring Willem Dafoe as Seppala and Diesel, one of Togo’s own descendants, in the lead role. And in 2022, a statue of Togo was unveiled in Maine, where he spent his final years. Seppala himself said it best: “I thought of the ice and the darkness and the terrible wind and the irony that men could build planes and ships. But when Nome needed life in little packages of serum, it took the dogs to bring it through.”
item.dc:creator: Joyika
